name_en: Rabbi Yisrael ben Eliezer, the Baal Shem Tov
bio: Founder of the chassidic movement. Son of Rabbi Eliezer, known for his extraordinary love of every Jew and his teaching that every person can serve G-d through joy and simple faith. His teachings became the spiritual foundation that the Alter Rebbe later developed into Chabad chassidus.

common_name: Rabbi Eliezer (father of Baal Shem Tov)
bio: Father of the Baal Shem Tov. Renowned for his extraordinary dedication to hachnasat orchim (hosting guests), using the vast majority of his wealth to host 20-30 poor and lonely guests every Shabbat. Before his passing, when his son Yisrael was about five years old, he left him two foundational commands: to fear no one but G-d, and to love every single Jew unconditionally. These became core principles of the chassidic movement his son would later establish.

name_en: Rabbi Tzvi, son of the Baal Shem Tov
name_he: רבי צבי בן הבעל שם טוב
common_name: Rabbi Tzvi (son of the Baal Shem Tov)
bio: Son of the Baal Shem Tov, primarily remembered for an extraordinary display of humility and self-nullification regarding the leadership of the chassidic movement.

name_en: Rabbi Dovber, the Maggid of Mezeritch
bio: Primary successor to the Baal Shem Tov and the teacher who systematized chassidic teaching. The Alter Rebbe was among his greatest disciples, and the journey of the Alter Rebbe to Mezeritch became foundational lore in Chabad.

A gaon, tzaddik, and devoted disciple of the Baal Shem Tov, who served as a special emissary of his master. He undertook a pivotal mission to Vilna to the Gaon Rabbi Shlomo Yochanan, conveying the Baal Shem Tov's message that his son Chaim Eliyahu should travel to study in Vitebsk.

common_name: Rabbi Baruch (father of the Alter Rebbe)
bio: Father of the Alter Rebbe. Known for his profound humility, self-imposed exile, and prophetic foresight regarding his son's greatness. Owned a farm where the hidden tzaddik R' Chaim Eliyahu would stay for weeks at a time, becoming a historical witness to the young Alter Rebbe's wondrous talents.

An outstanding disciple of the Baal Shem Tov, despite being the son of Rabbi Shlomo Yochanan, one of the great Torah geniuses of Vilna. After his father was visited by R' Shimon Zundel of Trisk on a mission from the Baal Shem Tov, he traveled to Vitebsk to study among the hidden tzaddikim there. Became a close friend of R' Baruch, the father of the Alter Rebbe, and a key witness to the Alter Rebbe's childhood greatness.

A prominent and highly respected member of his community who earned the title 'Baal Shem' (Master of the Name) because he engaged in providing spiritual remedies and healing incantations. Featured in a frightening, prophetic dream experienced by the Alter Rebbe at age ten in the synagogue of Liozna.

Founder of Chabad chassidus. Author of Tanya and the Shulchan Aruch HaRav. Disciple of the Maggid of Mezeritch. Established Chabad as a distinct intellectual stream of chassidus. Imprisoned by the Czarist government in 1798 and 1800; his release on Yud-Tes Kislev is celebrated as the 'Rosh Hashanah of Chassidus.' Died in 1812 while fleeing Napoleon's advance.

A chassid of the Alter Rebbe and older brother of Rabbi Zev Vilenker (his senior by twenty years). Was assigned by the Alter Rebbe, together with R' Moshe Meizlish, to a covert mission to edit and compile intelligence on the antisemitic writings of the Russian statesman Derzhavin, ultimately securing the safety of the Jewish community in Russia by influencing the Tsar to burn the hateful report.

Younger brother of R' Moshe Vilenker by twenty years; a chassid of the Alter Rebbe. Drawn to chassidus in the poor districts of Vilna by observing chassidim celebrating joyously over the persecution of a colleague. Famous for telling his older brother who asked for stories of miracles: 'I am giving you the marrow of the oil, and you are asking me for the toenails.' His home in Vitebsk continued to be a center for chassidic gatherings for generations.

One of the foremost intellectual thinkers among the students of the Alter Rebbe. A tremendous scholar in revealed Torah and chassidic philosophy. Earned his livelihood as a lumber merchant, famously found standing motionless in the middle of a busy lumber fair, deep in meditation on the greatness of the Creator. The Alter Rebbe declared that when he prayed 'Sim Shalom,' the Yechidah of his soul shone within him, higher than the Revelation of Elijah.

A prominent and highly respected chassid of the Alter Rebbe and the Mitteler Rebbe, who served as the Rabbi of Krāslava. Famous for his fiery passion during the Torah reading of Parshas Zachor—a sight that drew R' Binyamin Kletzker to the Alter Rebbe's movement. Traveled extensively to spread chassidus, sometimes ascending the synagogue bimah disguised as a traveling maggid.

A devoted chassid of the Alter Rebbe credited with sparking a historic spiritual revolution in the town of Dribin, successfully transforming its leading, staunchly opposed Torah scholars—including Rabbi Zerach Meir and his students—into devoted chassidim of the Alter Rebbe.

A tremendous Torah scholar (Talmid Chacham Atzum) who lived in Dribin during the era of the Alter Rebbe. A sixty-year-old sage who never ceased his Torah study for even a moment. Through the influence of R' Menachem Zecharia, he and his students transformed into devoted chassidim of the Alter Rebbe.

One of the greatest and most prominent chassidim of the Alter Rebbe and the Mitteler Rebbe, both of whom held him in great affection. A recognized Torah genius and serious intellectual who served as a chozer—responsible for memorizing, reviewing, and repeating the Alter Rebbe's chassidic discourses. Witness to the Alter Rebbe's extraordinary state of deveikus during the Torah reading of Bereishis.

A prominent and wealthy chassid of the Alter Rebbe and the Mitteler Rebbe, regarded as a true 'Baal Madrega' and considered to have achieved the spiritual rank of a Beinoni as defined in Tanya. Used his connections within the Russian royal court to assist the Rebbes and the Jewish community. Played a crucial role in locating the Alter Rebbe during his 1798 imprisonment in the St. Petersburg fortress by hiding a note in a plate of fruit preserve. The Alter Rebbe stayed at his spacious Petersburg home after his release.

A renowned chassid known for his extraordinary devotion, intense prayer, and total immersion in chassidic philosophy. Lived in Lepli, Belarus, earning a modest living as a salt merchant. Famously refused the Alter Rebbe's offer of a blessing for wealth, fearing it would distract him from divine service; accepted a blessing for longevity only on condition his life would be spiritually meaningful. Continued as a devoted follower of the Tzemach Tzedek.

A prominent historical figure who lived in Shvintzian (also associated with Vilna). A highly respected gaon, wealthy merchant, and initially one of the fiercest opponents of the chassidic movement before becoming connected to the Alter Rebbe.

name_en: Rabbi Dovber Schneuri, the Mitteler Rebbe
bio: Second Lubavitcher Rebbe. Son and successor of the Alter Rebbe. Established his court in Lubavitch in 1813. His chassidic discourses are considered 'pure chassidus'—the most expansive and detailed chassidic philosophy. Sent Reb Hillel Paritcher to spread chassidus through towns and villages. Arrested and released on Yud Kislev 1826, an event celebrated annually.

A towering Chabad chassid, profound Torah scholar, and exemplary 'Oved' (one whose divine service focuses on emotional refinement and prayer). Served as Rabbi of Paritch and later Bobruisk. Originally a follower of Rabbi Mordechai of Chernobyl before joining the Mitteler Rebbe's court. The Tzemach Tzedek categorized him as an 'Oved' in contrast to R' Yitzchak Isaac of Homel ('Maskil'). Famously said that after studying Tanya, he realized he was not even a Beinoni. Passed away in Kherson in 1864.

name_en: Rabbi Yitzchak Isaac Epstein of Homel
bio: A towering intellectual and one of the most prominent chassidim of the Alter Rebbe, the Mitteler Rebbe, and the Tzemach Tzedek. The Tzemach Tzedek categorized him as a 'Maskil' (intellectual), in contrast to his peer R' Hillel of Paritch ('Oved'). Served as Rabbi of Vitebsk. In his youth served as a badchan at weddings, dedicating earnings to support Jews in the Holy Land. Sent by the Tzemach Tzedek as emissary to Rabbi Yisrael of Ruzhin.

name_en: Rabbi Yitzchak Isaac of Vitebsk (era of the Tzemach Tzedek)
bio: An elder chassidic master prominent during the leadership of the Tzemach Tzedek. Traveled to Lubavitch for Shavuot 1857, among a massive gathering of over 120 rabbis and prominent elder chassidim, including R' Yitzchak Isaac of Homel and R' Hillel of Paritch.

name_en: Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneersohn, the Tzemach Tzedek
bio: Third Lubavitcher Rebbe. Grandson of the Alter Rebbe; son of Rabbi Shalom Shachna. Author of the responsa Tzemach Tzedek and numerous chassidic works. His sons established multiple Chabad dynasties including Lubavitch, Kapust, Niezhin, and Avritch. Established the surname 'Schneersohn' for his descendants.

Eldest son of the Tzemach Tzedek. A prominent and devoted figure who served his father faithfully until passing after a short illness, predeceasing his father. Father of Rabbi Zalman who was assigned 'weekly mitzvot' by the Tzemach Tzedek and was noted for his self-sacrifice.

Son of the Tzemach Tzedek. Established the Kapust branch of Chabad. Father of Rabbi Chaim Shlomo Zalman Yosef Schneersohn. The family surname 'Schneersohn' officially began with the Tzemach Tzedek's sons; the Maharil originally used the surname 'Schneuri'.

name_en: Rabbi Chaim Shlomo Zalman Yosef Schneersohn
name_he: רבי חיים שלמה זלמן יוסף שניאורסאהן
bio: Son of the Maharil of Kapust (Rabbi Yehuda Leib), grandson of the Tzemach Tzedek. Recognized from a young age for extraordinary spiritual level and intellectual depth. His grandfather, the Tzemach Tzedek, would assign him 'weekly mitzvot' along with his cousins.

A chassid recorded as having described his very first visit to the Tzemach Tzedek. Recalled the majestic moments of awe and glory he experienced when entering his first yechidus with the Rebbe alongside his father.

name_en: Rabbi Shneur Zalman Fradkin (Gaon of Lublin)
bio: Renowned as the Gaon of Lublin and the author of the halachic work Torat Chesed. A vast genius famous for his extraordinary memory—he could see a book briefly and know its contents by heart. A devoted chassid of the Tzemach Tzedek. The Tzemach Tzedek once asked him a Mishnah he could not remember, explaining the Gaon was in such a state of bittul that his ego had vanished. In his later years moved to Jerusalem and merited to teach the future Rebbe Rayatz.

Fourth Lubavitcher Rebbe. Youngest son of the Tzemach Tzedek and successor in Lubavitch. Famous for the motto 'L'chatchila Ariber'—leaping over obstacles from the start. Honored with writing the final words of a secret Torah scroll completed in Lubavitch in 1881.

A revered chassid and elder mashpia known for his profound insight and his extreme care in choosing his words. Worked in the timber trade. A meticulous transmitter of chassidic history, preserving accounts of the secret 1881 Torah scroll completion in Lubavitch and the Tzemach Tzedek's altering of the order of yechidus. Whispered at the funeral of R' Shilem Kuratin: 'For sixteen years he served the Holy One, blessed be He.'

name_en: Rabbi Sholom DovBer Schneersohn, the Rashab
bio: Fifth Lubavitcher Rebbe. Founded the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva network in 1897, considered one of his greatest legacies. Fled Lubavitch in 1915 during WWI to Rostov-on-Don. His chassidic discourses, especially the Hemshech Tarsav (1906) and Toras Shalom, are foundational. Sometimes called 'the Maimonides of Chassidus' for his systematic treatment of chassidic philosophy.

Wife of the Rashab and mother of the Rayatz. Active in the Rebbe Rashab's court. Asked about specific family relationships and traditions, including why the Rashab referred to her father in letters as 'my son-in-law' and another relative as 'my grandson.' Was present when chassidim came for yechidus and noted that whenever the Rashab received people, 'the floor is left wet with tears.'

A highly revered mashpia in the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva in Lubavitch. Renowned for absolute, uncompromising inner authenticity (pnimiyus), intense focus on the service of prayer, and profound dedication to G-d. The Rashab said of him after his passing: 'There is no one like Shilem,' and rejected proposed successors as not being 'pnimiyim like Shilem.' R' Alter Shimchovitz testified that he served G-d to the absolute maximum capacity that a created being could possibly reach.

name_en: Rabbi Yitzchak Horowitz, Reb Itche Masmid
name_he: רבי יצחק האראוויץ - ר' איטשע מתמיד
bio: A legendary chassid of the Rashab and the Rayatz, universally known as 'the diligent one' for his unparalleled diligence in Torah study. Served as a wandering emissary (Shadar) for the Rebbe Rayatz across Russia, Europe, and the United States to raise funds for Chabad institutions. Lived a life almost entirely detached from worldly matters—fasted frequently, ate one casual meal a day, barely slept. Murdered Al Kiddush Hashem in Riga in 1941/1942.

A highly revered and beloved chassid of the Rashab, the Rayatz, and the Lubavitcher Rebbe. Renowned for profound inner emotional service (pnimiyus), exactness in transmitting chassidic traditions, and fiery dedication to truth. Lost his wife and sons during the Communist Revolution and arrived in the Holy Land lonely and destitute. The Rashab called him an 'angel' and said his niggun 'comes from the forest.' Traveled to the Rebbe's court in New York in Adar 1947 and remained there until his passing on Erev Shavuos 1949.

Chief Rabbi of Bnei Brak in Israel. In his younger years served as the 'Rav of the Court' (Rav HaChatzer) for the Rashab, gaining unparalleled firsthand knowledge of the Rebbe's halachic rulings and personal customs. The Lubavitcher Rebbe frequently consulted him on the exact practices of the Rashab, including specifications for constructing a mikvah. Witnessed the only known photograph of the Rashab being taken in Rostov 1919/1920.

A legendary mashpia and scholar who served as a primary model for chassidic avoda. In 1912 selected by the Rashab among an elite group sent to Hebron to establish Yeshiva Torat Emet; later a central figure at the Jerusalem branch. Served as mashpia of the Chabad yeshiva in Warsaw during the interwar period; one of his prominent students was Rabbi Moshe Ashkenazi. A chozer of the Rashab's discourses. Refused a prestigious burial plot, insisting on being buried 'in the ground like everyone else.'

A towering chassidic figure known for profound emotional service, sharp and uncompromising truthfulness, and absolute devotion to the Rebbes of Chabad. Lived in Russia (including Nevel) and later in Tel Aviv and Kfar Chabad. Coined the phrase, 'Whoever does not have the current Rebbe, did not have the previous Rebbe either.' Major chassidic mentors of the next generation including R' Mendel Futerfas and R' Nissan Nemenov attributed their chassidic vitality directly to his influence. Passed away on 3 Shvat 1952.

name_en: Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn, the Rayatz
name_he: רבי יוסף יצחק שניאורסאהן - אדמו"ר הריי"צ
bio: Sixth Lubavitcher Rebbe. Son and successor of the Rashab. Led Chabad through the Soviet persecution; arrested by the OGPU in 1927 and sentenced to death, his release on Yud-Beis Tammuz is celebrated annually. Escaped through Riga, Warsaw, and Otwock; arrived in the United States in 1940 and established the Lubavitch headquarters at 770 Eastern Parkway in Brooklyn. Built underground networks across Soviet Russia through emissaries like R' Chonya Morozov, R' Simcha Gorodetsky, and R' Itche Masmid.

Father of the Lubavitcher Rebbe. A renowned Torah scholar, master of Kabbalah, and Chief Rabbi of Yekaterinoslav (Dnepropetrovsk) in Ukraine. His rabbinic ordination was granted by Rabbi Chaim of Brisk. The Rashab listed him among three specific chassidim about whom he said, 'When one of these three asks something, you have to know what to answer.' Courageously declared at a Soviet rabbinical conference that he would not sign a false document even if it meant giving up his life. Ultimately gave his life in actual mesiras nefesh under Soviet persecution.

Mother of the Lubavitcher Rebbe. Wife of Rabbi Levi Yitzchak Schneerson. Stood by her husband through exile and persecution under the Soviet regime. After his passing, she eventually was brought to Brooklyn where she lived near her son.

Brother of the Lubavitcher Rebbe; son of Rabbi Levi Yitzchak Schneerson. R' Simcha Gorodetsky witnessed him and his father completing an entire Talmudic tractate every single night during a visit to Yekaterinoslav. Later in life he frequently farbrenged with R' Nachum Goldschmid in Tel Aviv.

Personal secretary to the Rebbe Rayatz and manager of the entire underground Jewish network throughout the Soviet Union. A brilliant maskil in chassidus and a highly elevated servant of G-d. The Rashab gifted him with profound love and fear of G-d. On Tisha B'Av he would weep over the destruction of the Temple as if it were happening then; on Purim distributed Mishloach Manos all day with boundless joy. Martyred for his holy work under the Soviets (Hy"d).

A towering Chabad figure who served as Rosh Yeshiva and mashpia of the Chabad yeshivas in Tel Aviv and Rishon LeZion, where he also served as rabbi of the chassidic community. Renowned for profound Torah knowledge, sharpness, pedagogical mastery, and fierce dedication to authentic chassidic life. A smile never left his face, even during the most difficult times. Often told his students: 'We are all chassidim of the Rebbe, and I am merely the match that lights the fire.'

Legendary mashpia of the Tomchei Temimim yeshivas in Russia, Tel Aviv, Lod, and ultimately Kfar Chabad. The Rayatz appointed him mashpia in Rostov at age 25, telling him, 'This is a time of war. We take soldiers and make them officers.' The worst label he could give a person was 'Chitzon' (superficial). The Rebbe attested to his unmatched impact: 'The only place where one receives true chassidic grounding is by R' Shlomo Chaim.' Passed away 19 Iyar 5731 (1971).

A foundational figure in establishing the Chabad-Lubavitch community and its institutions in the Holy Land. Subdued his natural inclination toward private Torah study to fulfill the Rebbe's directives in public activism. Established a yeshiva and cheder in Tel Aviv per the Rayatz's directives. Succeeded R' Shlomo Yosef Zevin as rabbi of the Chabad synagogue on Montefiore Street. His Tel Aviv home (corner of Rothschild and Mazeh) was the nerve center of Chabad activities in Israel, called by R' Zusha Wilimovsky 'an institution of the Rebbe.'

A legendary mashpia and living symbol of mesiras nefesh. Chief organizer of the 1946 underground 'Exodus from Egypt' that smuggled hundreds of chassidic families out of the Soviet Union via Lviv. Captured and sentenced to eight years in a Siberian labor camp. Maintained strict kashrus and spiritual integrity throughout. Later served as primary mashpia in Kfar Chabad, shaping a generation of students through profound, raw, authentic chassidic teachings.

A towering chassidic figure, profound thinker, and devoted soldier of Chabad. Served as a primary mashpia in the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva and as personal secretary to the Rebbe Rayatz. Led the underground yeshiva network in Soviet Russia. Taught that the entirety of chassidic philosophy boiled down to one point: 'The world is not a reality at all.' Hy"d.

A central pillar of the Chabad community in Tel Aviv, a devoted emissary of the Rayatz in the Holy Land. Categorized by the Rebbe as one of the key figures who took part in the Rayatz's historic liberation. Passed away on 9 Iyar 1963 and was buried in Tzfat.

A prominent mashpia, central pillar of the Chabad community in Tel Aviv, and legendary expositor of chassidic philosophy. The Rayatz remarked that establishing the entire Tomchei Temimim yeshiva network would have been worthwhile even if only to produce Reb Nachum. Known as 'the explainer of chassidus to the masses'—his Tanya classes were broadcast on radio. Israel's President Zalman Shazar and author S.Y. Agnon attended his farbrengens. Dubbed 'Peh Mefik Margaliyot' (a mouth producing pearls) by Reb Itche Masmid.

A prominent chassid, profound intellectual in chassidus, and dedicated communal activist in the Holy Land. The Rayatz held him in extremely high regard, saying activists in the Holy Land must be 'ours—like Leizer [Karasik] and Meishke.' His home on Feierberg Street in Tel Aviv was the ultimate destination for anyone seeking to clarify difficult concepts in chassidus.

A prominent Chabad rabbi and author who immigrated to the Holy Land. Served as rabbi of the Neve Tzedek neighborhood in Jaffa, succeeding the veteran Chabad rabbi Rabbi Shneur Zalman Slonim. Remembered for deep wisdom, level-headed judgment, and profound dedication to teaching chassidic philosophy.

A prominent and deeply emotional chassid renowned for phenomenal memory, intense spiritual passion, and a foundational role in preserving and transmitting chassidic history. Father of R' Yoel Kahn, the Rebbe's chief chozer. Immigrated to the Holy Land in the 1930s, residing in Ramat Gan, Tel Aviv, and eventually Kfar Chabad. Suffered three years of exile in Siberia for studying Torah and spreading Judaism, maintaining a deep correspondence with R' Yankel Zurovitzer during that time.

A legendary and fiery chassid celebrated for his profound, uncompromising hiskashrus to the Rebbes of Chabad. As a Tomchei Temimim student around 1898 in Lubavitch, he secretly copied the Rashab's unreleased marginal notes on Torah Or—when discovered, the Rashab praised him saying, 'A blessing upon him, and I wish others would steal too!' Played a central role corresponding with the seventh Rebbe to plead with him to accept the leadership in 1951.

A highly revered and authentic chassid who embodied the core educational values of Tomchei Temimim—serving G-d and showing immense love for his fellow Jews in every situation, always out of joy and a good heart. Maintained close correspondence with R' Folleh Kahn during the latter's Siberian exile, sending him deep chassidic insights regarding prayer and the holidays. Martyred Al Kiddush Hashem under the Soviets; the Rebbe established his yahrzeit as the 24th of Teves.

A legendary chassid, devoted Shadar of the Rayatz, and towering symbol of mesiras nefesh who survived decades of communist persecution. In 1923 sent by the Rayatz to establish a network of underground Torah schools in the Bukhara region, gathering over 800 students under 45 teachers in Samarkand within three months. Endured 10 years of imprisonment and exile. Finally permitted to leave Russia in 1964 and moved to Israel, establishing a Talmud Torah for Bukharan immigrants in Kfar Chabad.

A scholarly and deeply devoted chassid whose life bridged Soviet Russia and the rebuilding of Chabad in Bnei Brak, Israel. Disabled by a leg wound from WWII military service, he used his condition as cover to teach Jewish children in an underground cheder in Tashkent (organized by R' Aharon Chazan). Known for immense Torah genius, fiery chassidic spirit, sharp wit, and self-sacrifice. After immigrating, became a primary speaker and spiritual pillar of the Chabad synagogue in Bnei Brak.

A prominent mashpia known for sharp intellect, profound connection to the Rebbe, and absolute precision in transmitting chassidic history. Initially served as mashpia in the Chabad Yeshiva in Kiryat Gat. In 1972 the Rebbe instructed him to establish a Yeshiva for Russian immigrants in Nachlat Har Chabad (Kiryat Malachi). A student of R' Mendel Futerfas. Provided profound chassidic guidance to the community following 3 Tammuz 1994.

A towering, legendary chassid considered by his peers from the original Lubavitch yeshiva to have reached the rare spiritual level of a Beinoni. Immigrated to the Holy Land in 1935, living in Jerusalem, Ramat Gan, and ultimately Bnei Brak. Despite his brilliant mind, chose to work as a simple construction worker so his mind would be free for Torah and chassidus. The Rebbe approved the inscription on his tombstone: 'He was careful in thought, speech, and action all the days of his life.'

A profound mashpia and influential figure who led a small but vibrant Chabad community in Ramat Gan. Known for immense success in translating deep chassidic concepts into accessible, modern language. Transmitted teachings from his Warsaw mashpia, Rabbi Alter Simchovitz, regarding the inner perfection of R' Shilem Kuratin.

A towering chassidic figure, devoted emissary, and central pillar of the Chabad community in Jerusalem. Born in the Holy Land, traveled to Lubavitch in his youth where he studied in Tomchei Temimim and received rare close attention from the Rebbes. A disciple of Reb Leizer Chechersker. During his youth regularly ate at the home of Rebbetzin Rivkah and on Shabbos and holidays at the table of the Rashab.

A legendary mashpia and principal of the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva in Brunoy, France. Universally revered for unparalleled, uncompromising kabbalas ol (acceptance of the Heavenly yoke) and intense daily practice of iskafya (subduing physical desires). His chassidic vitality was attributed to the profound influence of R' Zalman Moshe HaYitzchaki.

A deeply devoted chassid known for uncompromising dedication to Judaism and Jewish education under the oppressive Soviet regime. Managed an underground cheder in Russia; arrested in Elul 1937, imprisoned and tortured for over a year, miraculously released the day after Yom Kippur 1938. Upon learning his daughter had been sent to a Soviet school as a condition of his release, he declared he would return to prison immediately rather than subject her to communist secular education.

A central and highly influential figure in the early years of Kfar Chabad. Served as spiritual director (Menahel Ruchani) of the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva in Lod, credited with successfully raising a chassidic generation among Sephardic youth. Married to the daughter of R' Zalman Moshe HaYitzchaki. Moved to New York in 1959. Renowned as both a profound maskil and a dedicated oved.

A towering figure in Chabad history who seamlessly combined strict rabbinic authority with pure, authentic chassidishkeit. Eulogized by Rabbi Yaakov Landau as the 'Peri Etz Hadar' of Lubavitch. Before moving to the Holy Land served as rabbi in several Soviet cities and as director and mashpia in the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva in Shchedrin. Later served as rabbi of the Mea Shearim neighborhood in Jerusalem and as prominent Chabad rabbi and dayyan in Tel Aviv-Jaffa. Profoundly shaped the next generation of Chabad mentors including R' Chaim Shaul Brook and R' Shlomo Chaim Kesselman.

A prominent chassid and scholar who played a central role in establishing and nurturing the Chabad community in Jerusalem during the mid-20th century. Arrived in Israel in 1950 and served as rabbi for the Chabad community in Jerusalem, deepening chassidic life by encouraging study of chassidus, revitalizing the avoda of prayer, and connecting many to the Chabad Rebbes. After the passing of the Rayatz, boldly testified to the holiness of the seventh Rebbe: 'The Rebbe is a Rebbe!'

Son of Rabbi Nachum Shmaryahu Sasonkin; a legendary chassid remembered for fierce devotion, unyielding mesiras nefesh in Soviet Russia, and profound connection to the Lubavitcher Rebbe. Famously inspired teshuvah in a Jewish prisoner named Rozman by stepping forward and claiming to be him to spare him forced Shabbos work, then accepting solitary confinement under inhumane conditions. After miraculous release from Russia, moved to Jerusalem.

Served as rabbi of the Chabad community in Tel Aviv. Father of Rabbi Mordechai Shmuel Ashkenazi and son-in-law and successor of Rabbi Eliezer Karasik. Lived in his late father-in-law's home at the corner of Rothschild Boulevard and Maze Street in Tel Aviv. Educated by his great mashpia Rabbi Alter Shimchovitz. Acquired a special small Torah scroll that had a rich history in a Russian prison, which he brought as a gift to the Lubavitcher Rebbe, who used to dance with it during Hakafot of Simchat Torah.

A distinguished chassid and 'Tamim' at the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva in Lubavitch during the era of the Rashab and the Rayatz. Rabbi Yaakov Landau counted him among the most wondrous and spiritually abstracted students, calling him an 'outstanding Tamim.' During a yechidus the Rashab told him, 'From your question, I see that you have elevated yourself more than I thought.'

Widely known as 'the Chozer'—the individual responsible for memorizing, reviewing, and transcribing the Rebbe's discourses. Son of R' Folleh Kahn. A preserver of the Rebbe's teachings and transmitter of remarkable stories about the Rebbe's personal care for individuals. Regularly sent transcripts (hanachos) of the Rebbe's discourses to his father in Israel. A trusted guide for other chassidim.

A prominent 'man of Halacha' and the author of the well-known book Sipurei Chassidim (Chassidic Tales). Served as rabbi of the Chabad synagogue in Tel Aviv before R' Eliezer Karasik succeeded him. Famously used a chassidic story to block a Tel Aviv municipal law banning beggars. In an article commemorating the 150th anniversary of the Alter Rebbe's passing, described him as 'a wondrous figure who generated entire eras and revolutionized Jewish worldview, lifestyle, and feeling.'

A prominent chassid best known for significant contributions to chassidic literature and his role in preserving the oral traditions of the movement. Authored two influential collections: Shmuot VeSippurim (Hearings and Stories) and Lubavitch VeChayaleha (Lubavitch and Its Soldiers). The Rebbe personally encouraged him to write and publish these stories, emphasizing that chassidic stories can sometimes awaken fear of Heaven more effectively than other teachings.

A towering halachic authority and devoted chassid; author of Ketzot HaShulchan, Piskei HaSiddur, and Shiurei Tziyon. Established that an Amah is 48 centimeters and a Revi'it is 86.25 grams—standard halachic measurements in much of the Jewish world. Engaged in a famous dispute with the Chazon Ish over measurements, defending the Alter Rebbe's precision. Edited the Luach Kolel Chabad. Immediately after the Rayatz's passing, declared at a Jerusalem gathering: 'The golden chain will not break; the Ramash Shlita will continue it!'

Esteemed Mara D'asra (Chief Rabbi) of Kfar Chabad for twenty-five years, guiding the community with brilliance, firm authority, and absolute devotion to the Rebbe. Tragically killed on the night of 8 Sivan 1974 at age 94 when his vehicle overturned returning from Jerusalem to Kfar Chabad. The Rebbe dedicated an entire Sicha to this heartbreaking event. Succeeded as Mara D'asra by his son-in-law Rabbi Nachum Trebnik.

Arrived in the Holy Land in 1949 and was appointed Rabbi of the Chabad community in Lod and Rosh Yeshiva of the local Tomchei Temimim yeshiva. Despite severe financial hardship, rejected tempting offers for other rabbinic positions conditional upon joining 'Hapoel HaMizrachi.' Accepted the Lod position only after a direct written command from the Rayatz in Tishrei 5710.

A highly distinguished chassid and prominent student of the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva. The Rashab famously declared that the entire establishment of Tomchei Temimim was worthwhile just for the sake of producing two specific individuals: Rabbi David Horodoker and Rabbi Itche Masmid. A student of the elder chassid R' Michoel Bliner.

A prominent chassid who served as one of the great mashpiyim at the Toras Emes yeshiva in Jerusalem. Contributed to establishing and building the Chabad community in Jerusalem, alongside Rabbi David Goldberg and Rabbi Moshe Weber. Student and brother-in-law of R' Alter Shimchovitz; preserved and relayed many stories and teachings from his great mentor.

Served as a member of Agudas Chasidei Chabad in the United States. The designated figure responsible for handling complex and sensitive communal matters; in 1945 his expertise was required to deal with a severe problem in the Chicago Chabad community. A highly experienced fundraiser; Rabbi Shlomo Cunin accompanied him at the start of his shlichus specifically to learn the art of fundraising.

name_en: Rabbi Menachem Mendel Schneerson, the Lubavitcher Rebbe
bio: Seventh Lubavitcher Rebbe. Son of Rabbi Levi Yitzchak Schneerson and Rebbetzin Chana. Son-in-law of the Rayatz. Accepted the leadership of Chabad on 10 Shvat 1951. Built a global network of shluchim transforming Chabad into a worldwide movement. Edited and published the chassidic discourses and letters of the previous Rebbes. Suffered a heart attack in 1977 on Shemini Atzeret. Delivered the dramatic address of 28 Nissan 1991 calling on chassidim to 'do all you can to bring Moshiach.' Passed on 3 Tammuz 1994.

Wife of the Lubavitcher Rebbe and middle daughter of the Rayatz. Married the future Rebbe in Warsaw in 1928, an event the Rayatz officiated together with the leaders of the chassidic world. Together with her husband she fled Europe through Paris and Marseilles to New York. Known for her modesty, sharp intellect, and devotion to the Rebbe.

Arrived in the Holy Land in 1961 and became a central, inspiring figure in Kfar Chabad, where he worked as a melamed. Known for unceasing prayer and study, strict adherence to even the lightest customs, and ability to transmit the mesiras nefesh of Russian Jewry to younger generations. The Lubavitcher Rebbe instructed him to lay four pairs of Tefillin daily. Distinguished by his thick beard and Russian 'kasket' cap—the Rebbe instructed him not to replace it with a fedora as it reminded heaven of the self-sacrifice of Russian Jewry.
